what_it_does: Implements an address-mover that traverses the crossings graph stored in a SQLite links table along two axes: time via precedes edges and scale via contains edges. It accepts a canonical address, axis, direction, and optional limit, returns adjacent addresses verbatim in the shared address grammar, optionally resolves a gist for exchange nodes, and provides a recall-to-navigate composition helper.
observations: Only the two forward edges actually written by buildCrossings are used: precedes for time and contains for scale.
A MOVES dispatch table maps each valid (axis, direction) pair to an edge_type, match column, and emit column; invalid pairs throw.
Traversing the inverse direction is done by swapping match/emit on the same written edge, never by querying succeeds or is_contained_by.
The kind of an adjacent node is derived generically from the URI scheme prefix of its address.
Gist resolution is optional and only for exchange nodes, joining back to the exchanges table with the same pattern as whatTouchedFile.
navigateFromQuery composes recall (semantic/structural entry-point) with navigate (mechanical mover), defaulting recall to text mode for a model-free chain.
A valid move with no matching edge returns an empty array rather than throwing.
standouts: The MOVES registry is the central crux: it makes the allowed moves explicit and rejects cross-axis combinations in one place.
The deliberate rule against querying unwritten inverse edges is heavily documented and shapes every query.
The recall -> navigate chain demonstrates end-to-end composition from query to adjacent addresses.
conventions: Heavy JSDoc explaining intent, constraints, and meet-points with other files.
Canonical address grammar treated as the sole contract with project()/composition.
Fail-loud for unknown registry entries; empty-but-valid results for graph leaves.
Prepared SQL statements and parameterized WHERE clauses for the link traversal.
Type exports for the public surface.
concerns: The LIMIT clause is concatenated into SQL after Math.floor(k); while k is a number, string interpolation still bypasses the prepared-statement boundary.
Gist resolution constructs the exchange address by concatenating 'exchange://' || COALESCE(session_id, id) || '/' || line_start, which is brittle if the address grammar or schema changes.
navigateFromQuery awaits recall but then calls the synchronous navigate, mixing async/sync without an obvious reason beyond recall's signature.
There is no check that the entry address's scheme is sensible for the chosen axis; callers can request scale:in on a project and simply get [].
Ordering by links.ts assumes the column exists and is meaningful for both temporal and scale edges.
notes: A mechanical graph-mover over the crossings links table that treats the address string as the only contract with the rest of the front interface.
questions: What is the exact schema of the links table, including ts, edge_type, and id columns?
Is session_id nullable in exchanges, and does COALESCE(session_id, id) always reproduce the session id used in exchange:// addresses?
What does recall return when mode is text versus vector, and is provenance always a single canonical address?
Are there tests covering leaf cases and invalid axis/direction pairs?
How are project:// and session:// ids formatted, and do they ever collide with the scheme parser?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to move addresses over the beat-3 crossings graph along time and scale, and the implementation does exactly that using the documented edge vocabulary.
apparent_themes: Mechanical traversal over a persisted graph
Model-free composition of recall and navigation
Optional enrichment without making it part of the address contract
intention_signals: It is trying to make navigation across the ledger explicit, bounded, and reversible by encoding every legal move in a dispatch table, while keeping the address string as the only shared token between finding, moving, and resolving content.
novelty: The explicit decision to traverse only written forward edges in both directions by swapping match/emit, rather than relying on inverse edge rows that the file says are never populated.
contribution: A concrete, registry-based mover that emits and consumes canonical addresses, enabling time/scale traversal over the existing crossings graph without introducing new graph construction or model dependencies.
summary_for_embedding: navigate.ts implements deterministic address traversal over a SQLite crossings graph along temporal and scale adjacency edges, returning adjacent canonical addresses and composing with recall for query-driven navigation.
intention_for_embedding: This file exists to let callers move a canonical address forward/backward in time or up/down in scale, then optionally resolve a preview, forming a query-to-neighbour chain with recall.

symbols.does: Declares the two supported traversal axes: time and scale.
symbols.description: A string-literal union type that names the axes the mover can travel along: time for precedes edges and scale for contains edges.

symbols.does: Declares the four movement directions: next, prev, out, and in.
symbols.description: A string-literal union type for direction within an axis; next/prev apply to time and out/in apply to scale, though the pair is validated elsewhere.

symbols.does: Defines the input shape for a navigate call.
symbols.description: An interface carrying the entry address, chosen axis, direction, and an optional limit k; the address is expected to be verbatim in the shared address grammar.

symbols.does: Defines the output shape for one adjacent node.
symbols.description: An interface returning the adjacent canonical address, echoed axis and direction, a kind derived from the address scheme, and an optional gist for exchange nodes.

symbols.does: Extracts the scheme prefix from an address as its kind.
symbols.description: A small helper that splits an address on :// and returns the left side, falling back to the whole string if no separator is present; used to classify nodes generically.

symbols.does: Performs one mechanical move over the crossings graph.
symbols.description: Looks up the (axis, direction) pair in the MOVES registry, runs a parameterized SQL query against the links table to fetch the adjacent node ids, optionally joins back to exchanges for a gist, and returns the hits in address-grammar form.

symbols.does: Composes recall entry-point lookup with navigate movement.
symbols.description: Calls recall to obtain an entry address, guards against empty recall results, then invokes navigate on that address; defaults recall to text mode so the chain stays model-free.
